name: Business Media Literacy
description: Develop critical thinking about media, information, and communication in business contexts — evaluating sources, understanding media influence, content creation, and navigating the digital information landscape.

modules.title: What Is Media Literacy?
modules.objectives: Define media literacy; identify types of media and media ownership structures; describe the information ecosystem and explain why media literacy matters in personal and professional life.
modules.readingNote: News Literacy Project — What Is News Literacy?
modules.videoNote: Crash Course Media Literacy #1 — Media Literacy
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How to choose your news
modules.practiceNote: News Literacy Project — Checkology Virtual Classroom (free modules)
modules.assignment: Map your personal information ecosystem: list every media source you used in the past week (social media, TV, podcasts, websites). Categorize each by type and identify who owns it. Write a 200-word reflection on what this reveals.

modules.title: How Media Messages Are Constructed
modules.objectives: Explain framing, agenda-setting, and gatekeeping; identify media bias in news stories; distinguish spin from factual reporting.
modules.readingNote: News Literacy Project — Resource Library (Bias & Framing resources)
modules.videoNote: Crash Course Media Literacy #2 — Influence & Persuasion
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How false news can spread
modules.practiceNote: AllSides Media Bias Ratings — Compare coverage of the same story across outlets
modules.assignment: Select one current news story and find three articles covering it from outlets with different bias ratings. Identify framing choices, loaded language, and what facts each article omits. Write a 250-word analysis.

modules.title: Evaluating Sources & Fact-Checking
modules.titleZh: 评估信息来源与事实核查
modules.objectives: Apply the SIFT method (Stop, Investigate, Find better coverage, Trace claims); use lateral reading and fact-checking tools to verify information; distinguish news from opinion.
modules.readingNote: News Literacy Project — The SIFT Method
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Evaluating Sources in a Data-Driven World
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Media Literacy #3 — Navigating Digital Information
modules.practiceNote: FactCheck.org — Practice lateral reading with current fact-checks
modules.assignment: Use SIFT to evaluate five claims you encounter online this week. For each, document your investigation steps, the fact-checking tools used, and your conclusion about reliability. Submit a one-page report.

modules.title: Misinformation & Disinformation
modules.objectives: Distinguish misinformation from disinformation and malinformation; identify fake news characteristics, deepfake red flags, and propaganda techniques; analyze how misinformation goes viral.
modules.videoNote: Crash Course Media Literacy #4 — Misinformation & Disinformation
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How to spot a misleading graph
modules.practiceNote: Bad News Game — Interactive simulation of building a disinformation campaign
modules.assignment: Research one viral misinformation case (e.g., a health myth, a stock market rumor). Trace how it spread, identify the propaganda techniques used, and explain what a business professional should do before sharing such content.

modules.title: Advertising & Persuasion Techniques
modules.objectives: Identify common advertising appeals (ethos, pathos, logos); recognize emotional manipulation and native advertising; analyze influencer marketing ethics.
modules.readingNote: News Literacy Project — Advertising & Sponsored Content
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How the food industry tricks you with science
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Analyzing Purpose and Point of View in texts
modules.assignment: Collect five ads from different media channels. For each, identify the appeal type, target audience, persuasion technique, and whether it is transparent about being an advertisement. Present findings in a comparison table.

modules.title: Social Media Algorithms & Filter Bubbles
modules.titleZh: 社交媒体算法与信息茧房
modules.objectives: Explain how recommendation algorithms shape content feeds; define echo chambers and filter bubbles; analyze the link between algorithmic curation and political polarization.
modules.readingNote: News Literacy Project — Social Media & Algorithms
modules.videoNote: Crash Course Media Literacy #6 — Social Media
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — Beware online filter bubbles (Eli Pariser)
modules.assignment: Conduct a one-week algorithm audit on one social media platform. Note the types of content recommended to you, how recommendations shift when you interact with different content, and write a 200-word reflection on your personal filter bubble.

modules.title: Business News & Financial Media
modules.objectives: Read and interpret financial news articles and analyst reports; understand how earnings calls are communicated; critically compare media narratives to actual financial data.
modules.readingNote: News Literacy Project — Reading Business and Financial News Critically
modules.videoNote: Crash Course Business: Finance #1 — Introduction to Business Finance
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How does the stock market work?
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Stocks and Bonds (Core Finance)
modules.assignment: Find a recent earnings report from a publicly traded company. Read the CFO's press release, then find two news articles covering the same earnings. Write a 300-word comparison analyzing whether the media coverage accurately reflects the actual data.

modules.title: Data Journalism & Infographics
modules.objectives: Explain how data is used in journalistic storytelling; identify misleading data visualizations; create and evaluate responsible infographics.
modules.readingNote: News Literacy Project — Data Literacy & Visualizations
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How to spot a misleading graph
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Displaying and Describing Data
modules.assignment: Find three infographics from business news websites. For each, identify the data source, assess whether the visualization is misleading, and suggest one improvement. Then create your own simple infographic on a business topic of your choice.

questions.question: Which of the following best defines media literacy?
options: The ability to read and write printed text
The ability to access, analyze, evaluate, and create media in various forms
Knowing how to operate media equipment
Following verified news accounts on social media
questions.answer: The ability to access, analyze, evaluate, and create media in various forms
questions.explanation: Media literacy encompasses accessing, analyzing, evaluating, and creating media — not just consuming it.

questions.question: Agenda-setting theory suggests that media:
options: Tells audiences what to think about
questions.answer: Tells audiences what to think about
questions.explanation: Agenda-setting refers to media's power to influence which issues the public considers important, not the specific opinions held.

questions.question: The SIFT method's 'I' stands for:
questions.answer: Investigate the source
questions.explanation: SIFT: Stop, Investigate the source, Find better coverage, Trace claims to the original context.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "What distinguishes disinformation from misinformation" is: Disinformation is deliberately false; misinformation is false but shared without intent to deceive.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Disinformation is deliberately false; misinformation is false but shared without intent to deceive is correct.

questions.question: Which advertising appeal primarily uses logic, statistics, and evidence to persuade?
questions.answer: Logos
questions.explanation: Logos appeals to reason and logic; pathos appeals to emotion; ethos appeals to credibility.

questions.question: A filter bubble is best described as:
options: A personalized information environment that limits exposure to diverse viewpoints
A software tool that blocks spam
questions.answer: A personalized information environment that limits exposure to diverse viewpoints
questions.explanation: Filter bubbles are created by algorithms that tailor content to past behavior, narrowing the range of perspectives a user sees.

questions.question: When reading a company's earnings press release, a media-literate professional should:
options: Accept the figures as fully objective facts
Cross-reference with the actual financial filing and independent analyst reports
Avoid financial media entirely as it is always biased
questions.answer: Cross-reference with the actual financial filing and independent analyst reports
questions.explanation: Press releases are crafted by corporate communications teams and may emphasize favorable data; independent sources provide a more complete picture.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Native advertising differs from traditional advertising primarily because it:" is: Is always free for consumers.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Is designed to resemble editorial or organic content.

questions.question: Which technique is lateral reading?
options: Reading an article from top to bottom slowly
Opening new browser tabs to check what others say about a source
Reading the same article multiple times
Comparing the article to your prior knowledge
questions.answer: Opening new browser tabs to check what others say about a source
questions.explanation: Lateral reading means leaving the source to quickly search what credible third parties say about it — a key skill used by professional fact-checkers.

questions.question: A news article that selectively presents facts to support one conclusion is using:
questions.answer: Framing
questions.explanation: Framing involves emphasizing certain aspects of a story to promote a particular interpretation while downplaying others.

questions.question: Echo chambers on social media primarily result from:
options: Users being blocked by platform moderators
Algorithms and user choices that reinforce existing beliefs
Too many news sources being available
questions.answer: Algorithms and user choices that reinforce existing beliefs
questions.explanation: Echo chambers form when algorithms promote content aligned with existing preferences, and users actively seek confirming views, creating self-reinforcing information loops.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following would raise the most concern about a news source's credibility" is: The website has no 'About Us' page and no citations.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. The website has no 'About Us' page and no citations is correct.

questions.question: Which statement about opinion pieces is correct?
options: Opinion pieces follow the same fact-checking standards as news reports
Opinion pieces express the author's viewpoint and are not held to the same objectivity standards as news reports
Opinion pieces are always labeled as advertisements
Opinion pieces are only found in broadcast media
questions.answer: Opinion pieces express the author's viewpoint and are not held to the same objectivity standards as news reports
questions.explanation: News reports aim for objectivity; opinion pieces are explicitly subjective and represent the author's or publication's perspective.

questions.question: Gatekeeping in journalism refers to:
options: Blocking readers from accessing paywalled content
The process by which editors and platforms decide what information reaches the public
Verifying user identities on social media platforms
questions.answer: The process by which editors and platforms decide what information reaches the public
questions.explanation: Gatekeeping is the selection process — editors, algorithms, and platforms act as 'gatekeepers' controlling which stories get published and amplified.

questions.question: A colleague forwards you a viral social media post claiming a major company is about to go bankrupt, with no source cited. Describe the steps you would take using the SIFT method before deciding whether to share or act on this information.
questions.answer: Stop before sharing or acting; Investigate the source of the post and the claim; Find better coverage by searching reputable financial news outlets and the company's investor relations page; Trace the claim to its original context to see if it is based on real financial data or is fabricated.
questions.explanation: SIFT provides a systematic framework for evaluating unverified claims before spreading or acting on potentially false information.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "The information ecosystem refers to:" is: Only digital news platforms.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: The complete network of people, platforms, and practices that produce and distribute information.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of agenda-setting?
options: A newspaper endorsing a political candidate
A TV network devoting 80% of its coverage to inflation, making viewers rank it as the top national issue
A reporter giving false information about a corporation
A social media platform removing violent content
questions.answer: A TV network devoting 80% of its coverage to inflation, making viewers rank it as the top national issue
questions.explanation: Agenda-setting occurs when media emphasis on a topic increases the public's perceived importance of that topic.

questions.question: Which resource is best suited for lateral reading when fact-checking a news claim?
options: Reading the same article more carefully
Searching what other credible outlets and fact-checkers report about the same claim
Asking a friend for their opinion
questions.answer: Searching what other credible outlets and fact-checkers report about the same claim
questions.explanation: Lateral reading means stepping outside the source to verify it through independent, credible third parties.

questions.question: A deepfake poses a significant business risk primarily because:
options: They are too expensive to produce
They can fabricate realistic audio or video of executives making false statements
They are easily detectable by all viewers
questions.answer: They can fabricate realistic audio or video of executives making false statements
questions.explanation: Deepfakes can damage corporate reputations by spreading convincing but entirely fabricated content, making verification skills essential.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "An influencer posts a glowing review of a product without disclosing they were paid. This violates:" is: FTC endorsement guidelines requiring disclosure of material connections.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. FTC endorsement guidelines requiring disclosure of material connections is correct.

questions.question: Recommendation algorithms primarily optimize for:
questions.answer: User engagement (clicks, watch time, shares)
questions.explanation: Recommendation algorithms are designed to maximize engagement metrics, which can favor sensational or emotionally triggering content over accuracy.

questions.question: When a company's stock drops 5% the day after a strong earnings report, a media-literate investor would first:
options: Assume the media is wrong and hold the stock
Sell immediately based on the news headline
Investigate whether the market had priced in higher expectations
questions.answer: Investigate whether the market had priced in higher expectations
questions.explanation: Stock prices reflect expectations, not just results. Media-literate investors look beyond headlines to understand market context.

questions.question: A bar chart that starts its y-axis at 80 instead of 0 is an example of:
options: A misleading visualization that exaggerates differences
questions.answer: A misleading visualization that exaggerates differences
questions.explanation: Truncating a y-axis makes small differences appear dramatically larger than they are, misleading the audience.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "A well-crafted corporate press release in a crisis situation should:" is: Deny all wrongdoing and blame the media.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Acknowledge the issue, express concern, and outline steps being taken.

questions.question: Which SEO practice is most ethical and sustainable for business content?
options: Creating genuinely useful, well-researched content that earns organic links
questions.answer: Creating genuinely useful, well-researched content that earns organic links
questions.explanation: Ethical SEO (also called 'white hat' SEO) focuses on quality content that earns rankings naturally rather than manipulating algorithms.

questions.question: Which characteristic makes podcasting effective as a business communication tool?
options: It is entirely free to produce
It builds intimate, long-form relationships with an audience through voice and storytelling
It reaches more people than any other medium
It requires no planning or scripting
questions.answer: It builds intimate, long-form relationships with an audience through voice and storytelling
questions.explanation: Podcasts are consumed during commutes and leisure time, creating a personal connection that builds brand loyalty and thought leadership.

questions.question: Using a copyrighted image in a business blog post without permission or attribution is:
options: Acceptable as long as the image is credited
Legal under all fair use provisions
Acceptable if the post is non-commercial
questions.answer: Potentially copyright infringement
questions.explanation: Copyright law protects original works; using them without license or qualifying fair use is infringement regardless of commercial intent or credit.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "A global marketing team discovers that a campaign effective in the US is seen as offensive in Japan. This illustrates the importance of:" is: Cross-cultural media literacy and cultural sensitivity in communications.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Cross-cultural media literacy and cultural sensitivity in communications is correct.

questions.question: In the final Media Literacy Project, a student analyzing a company's PR campaign should prioritize:
options: Judging whether the product is good quality
Evaluating source credibility, framing, persuasion techniques, and accuracy of data claims
Counting how many times the brand name appears
Determining which platform had the most followers
questions.answer: Evaluating source credibility, framing, persuasion techniques, and accuracy of data claims
questions.explanation: Comprehensive media analysis applies the full toolkit of media literacy: source credibility, framing, rhetorical techniques, and data verification.

questions.question: Propaganda techniques often exploit:
options: Emotional appeals, repetition, and in-group versus out-group thinking
questions.answer: Emotional appeals, repetition, and in-group versus out-group thinking
questions.explanation: Propaganda bypasses critical thinking by targeting emotions and tribal identities, often repeating simple messages to make them feel true.

questions.question: Which statement about data journalism is most accurate?
options: Data is always objective and cannot be manipulated in journalism
Choosing what data to highlight and how to visualize it involves editorial judgment that can mislead
Infographics are always more accurate than written reports
Data journalism only applies to financial news
questions.answer: Choosing what data to highlight and how to visualize it involves editorial judgment that can mislead
questions.explanation: Data selection, framing, and visualization design are all editorial choices that can distort meaning even when the numbers themselves are accurate.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Media consolidation — when a few large corporations own most media outlets — is a concern for media literacy because it:" is: Increases the diversity of editorial perspectives available.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Can reduce the range of viewpoints and create conflicts of interest in coverage.

questions.question: Defamation in a business media context occurs when:
options: A journalist writes a negative but factually accurate story about a company
A false statement of fact is published that damages a person's or company's reputation
A company refuses to comment on a news story
An opinion piece criticizes a CEO's leadership style
questions.answer: A false statement of fact is published that damages a person's or company's reputation
questions.explanation: Defamation requires a false statement of fact (not opinion), published to third parties, causing reputational harm. Truth is an absolute defense.

questions.question: A startup you work for is releasing a new product next month. Your manager asks you to develop a content plan covering a blog post, a short video, and a social media strategy. Outline the key considerations for each format, including audience, tone, SEO, and ethical transparency.
questions.answer: Blog post: write for the target customer, use keywords naturally, structure with headings for SEO, cite sources, and clearly label any sponsored content. Short video: hook viewers in the first 5 seconds, tell a story rather than just listing features, include captions for accessibility, disclose if paid endorsers appear. Social media: tailor message length and tone to each platform's norms, disclose any paid partnerships per FTC rules, and engage authentically rather than using automated deceptive tactics.
questions.explanation: Effective business content creation requires audience awareness, format-specific best practices, and ethical transparency across all channels.

questions.question: You are preparing a market research report for your team and want to include data from three different countries. Describe two specific challenges cross-cultural media literacy poses for interpreting international business data and explain how you would address each challenge.
questions.answer: Challenge 1: Cultural framing — data may be presented through a local lens that reflects different values or economic priorities. Address by finding primary sources (government statistics, local industry bodies) rather than relying solely on Western-media interpretations. Challenge 2: Language and translation bias — key terms may not translate directly, and machine translation can lose nuance. Address by consulting bilingual professionals or localized market research firms to ensure accurate interpretation.
questions.explanation: Cross-cultural media literacy requires recognizing that information is always embedded in cultural context, and that accurate international analysis demands culturally-informed sourcing and interpretation.

quizQuestions.question: Media literacy is best defined as the ability to:
options: Access, analyze, evaluate, and create media
Follow news accounts on social media
quizQuestions.answer: Access, analyze, evaluate, and create media
quizQuestions.explanation: Media literacy is a set of skills for engaging critically with all forms of media, not just consuming them.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT a type of media?
options: Conversation between two friends with no recording
quizQuestions.answer: Conversation between two friends with no recording
quizQuestions.explanation: Media involves channels that store or transmit messages to audiences; a private unrecorded conversation is interpersonal communication, not media.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Media literacy is best defined as the ability to:" is: Access, analyze, evaluate, and create media.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Access, analyze, evaluate, and create media is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Framing in media refers to:
options: The physical borders around a TV screen
Emphasizing certain aspects of a story to shape how audiences interpret it
Writing balanced reports with no opinions
quizQuestions.answer: Emphasizing certain aspects of a story to shape how audiences interpret it
quizQuestions.explanation: Framing involves selecting which facts to highlight and how to contextualize them, influencing the audience's understanding without necessarily lying.

quizQuestions.question: Agenda-setting theory argues that media primarily:
options: Tells people what opinions to hold
Determines which topics people consider important
quizQuestions.answer: Determines which topics people consider important
quizQuestions.explanation: Agenda-setting theory holds that media does not tell us what to think, but it does influence what we think about by choosing what to cover.

quizQuestions.question: Gatekeeping in journalism is the process of:
options: Selecting which information gets published or broadcast
Charging subscription fees for news access
quizQuestions.answer: Selecting which information gets published or broadcast
quizQuestions.explanation: Gatekeepers — editors, platform algorithms, and publishers — decide what content reaches audiences.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Framing in media refers to:" is: Emphasizing certain aspects of a story to shape how audiences interpret it.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Emphasizing certain aspects of a story to shape how audiences interpret it is correct.

quizQuestions.question: The 'S' in the SIFT method stands for:
quizQuestions.answer: Stop
quizQuestions.explanation: SIFT: Stop before sharing, Investigate the source, Find better coverage, Trace claims to their original context.

quizQuestions.question: Lateral reading involves:
options: Reading an article from the bottom up
Leaving the source page to check what others say about it
Comparing two articles on the same page
quizQuestions.answer: Leaving the source page to check what others say about it
quizQuestions.explanation: Professional fact-checkers use lateral reading to quickly verify sources by searching for third-party information about them.

quizQuestions.question: FactCheck.org, Snopes, and PolitiFact are examples of:
quizQuestions.answer: Independent fact-checking organizations
quizQuestions.explanation: These are established fact-checking platforms that independently verify claims made in news and social media.

quizQuestions.question: Misinformation is defined as false information that is shared:
quizQuestions.answer: Without knowing it is false
quizQuestions.explanation: Misinformation is false information spread without deliberate intent to deceive, unlike disinformation which is intentionally deceptive.

quizQuestions.question: Disinformation differs from misinformation in that it is:
options: Deliberately created and spread to deceive
quizQuestions.answer: Deliberately created and spread to deceive
quizQuestions.explanation: Disinformation is intentionally false content created to mislead, manipulate, or harm — intent is the key distinguishing factor.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a propaganda technique?
options: Using fear appeals and us-versus-them messaging
Disclosing the author's potential conflicts of interest
quizQuestions.answer: Using fear appeals and us-versus-them messaging
quizQuestions.explanation: Fear appeals and in-group/out-group thinking are classic propaganda tools that bypass rational analysis.

quizQuestions.question: Deepfakes are a concern in business because they can:
options: Fabricate convincing false statements by real executives or public figures
Be detected instantly by all modern devices
quizQuestions.answer: Fabricate convincing false statements by real executives or public figures
quizQuestions.explanation: Deepfakes use AI to create realistic but fabricated audio or video, which can spread market-moving or reputation-damaging misinformation.

quizQuestions.question: An ad showing a celebrity endorsing a product uses which appeal?
quizQuestions.answer: Ethos
quizQuestions.explanation: Ethos appeals to credibility or authority — using a trusted or famous figure to lend credibility to a product.

quizQuestions.question: Native advertising is content that:
options: Is produced by local media outlets only
Is paid advertising designed to look like editorial content
quizQuestions.answer: Is paid advertising designed to look like editorial content
quizQuestions.explanation: Native advertising blends in with non-advertising content in style and format, making it harder for readers to recognize as paid promotion.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "An ad showing a celebrity endorsing a product uses which appeal" is: Ethos.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Ethos is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Native advertising is content that:" is: Is produced by local media outlets only.
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Is paid advertising designed to look like editorial content.

quizQuestions.question: A filter bubble is created when:
options: A user blocks all political content
Algorithms show users only content that aligns with their past behavior and preferences
quizQuestions.answer: Algorithms show users only content that aligns with their past behavior and preferences
quizQuestions.explanation: Filter bubbles are algorithmic environments that reduce exposure to diverse perspectives by personalizing content based on engagement history.

quizQuestions.question: An echo chamber primarily reinforces:
options: A wide range of political views
Existing beliefs by surrounding the user with like-minded content and people
quizQuestions.answer: Existing beliefs by surrounding the user with like-minded content and people
quizQuestions.explanation: Echo chambers, whether digital or social, amplify existing beliefs by limiting exposure to contradicting viewpoints.

quizQuestions.question: Social media algorithms primarily optimize content recommendations for:
options: User engagement metrics like clicks and watch time
quizQuestions.answer: User engagement metrics like clicks and watch time
quizQuestions.explanation: Engagement-based algorithms prioritize content that keeps users active on the platform, regardless of accuracy or quality.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "A filter bubble is created when:" is: Algorithms show users only content that aligns with their past behavior and preferences.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Algorithms show users only content that aligns with their past behavior and preferences is correct.

quizQuestions.question: A company reports $1 billion in revenue but a $200 million net loss. A media-literate reader would:
options: Conclude the company is performing excellently based on revenue
Look at both revenue and profitability, and understand the difference between them
Only trust the CEO's statement about the results
quizQuestions.answer: Look at both revenue and profitability, and understand the difference between them
quizQuestions.explanation: Revenue (total sales) and net profit (revenue minus all costs) are very different metrics; a business can have high revenue and still be unprofitable.

quizQuestions.question: An analyst report has a 'Buy' rating on a stock, but the firm also has an investment banking relationship with that company. This is a:
options: Potential conflict of interest that could bias the recommendation
Sign the analyst is highly credible
quizQuestions.answer: Potential conflict of interest that could bias the recommendation
quizQuestions.explanation: Investment banking relationships create incentives for analysts to issue favorable ratings, which is a documented source of bias in financial media.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "A company reports $1 billion in revenue but a $200 million net loss. A media-literate reader would:" is: Look at both revenue and profitability, and understand the difference between them.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Look at both revenue and profitability, and understand the difference between them is correct.

quizQuestions.question: A y-axis that begins at 50 instead of 0 on a bar chart will make differences look:
quizQuestions.answer: Larger than they actually are
quizQuestions.explanation: Truncating the y-axis compresses the scale and exaggerates the visual difference between bars.

quizQuestions.question: Data journalism uses data to:
options: Support, illustrate, and tell evidence-based news stories
Generate automated articles without human oversight
quizQuestions.answer: Support, illustrate, and tell evidence-based news stories
quizQuestions.explanation: Data journalism combines statistical analysis with narrative storytelling to make complex information accessible and verifiable.

quizQuestions.question: When evaluating an infographic, which question is most important to ask first?
options: Where does the data come from, and is the source credible?
Does it match my existing beliefs?
Was it shared by a popular account?
quizQuestions.answer: Where does the data come from, and is the source credible?
quizQuestions.explanation: Source credibility is the foundation of evaluating any data visualization — even perfectly designed infographics can mislead with faulty data.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "A y-axis that begins at 50 instead of 0 on a bar chart will make differences look:" is: Larger than they actually are.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Larger than they actually are is correct.
